people.bio:
Alfred Cleaver (b. c. 1834; d. 27 August 1909) was from a lace-making family. By 1862 he was in partnership with his brother Francis Frederick Cleaver. They ran a lace dressing, dyeing and bleaching works on Wilford Road under the name of FF and A Cleaver and Co. Other partners in the business included Henry Massey Bishop (until 1865) and William Mayo (until 1870). The partnership between Francis Frederick and Alfred Cleaver was dissolved on 15 March 1881. Alfred Cleaver and Co. carried on business as lace dressers, lace dyers and bleachers and finishers at Lenton. Richard Landor Warham left his partnership with Cleaver in this firm on 30 June 1895.

Alfred Cleaver contributed to the 2nd Report of the Children's Employment Commission, 1862, commenting that a limitation of the hours for workers under 18 would make no difference if applied across the trade, and that if the hours were limited at night he would begin at 6 and find no difficulty.

He married Annie Bennett (1838–1869) in 1863. Their only son was Robert Clifton Cleaver (b. 1868; d. 1 October 1900, Brandon, Manitoba, Canada). Alfred then married Mary Adelaide Allen (1841–1901) in 1874.

Alfred then married Mary Adelaide Allen (1841–1901) in 1874. Mary was the daughter of Richard Allen (b. Leicester, 1814; d. Nottingham, 1884) and Mary Ann Deacon (b. Boston, Lincolnshire, c.1811; d. Nottingham, 1900), who married in Basford in 1838. The Allen family resided at Albert Villa, 21 Cavendish Crescent South, The Park.

Richard Allen owned Richard Allen & Son, based at Caxton House, 34 Long Row East — bookbinders, booksellers, wholesale, mercantile and fancy stationers, photographers, steam printers, lithographers, engravers, account book manufacturers, paper merchants, pasteboard manufacturers, lace pattern card makers and box makers. The company printed and published a wide variety of books including Allen's Railway Time Table, Allen's Illustrated First Book or Reading Made Easy for Children, Allen's New and Improved Cricket Register (an early cricket scoring book), a range of historical charts and a number of local interest items, including Allen's Illustrated Hand-Book and Guide to all the Places of Interest in Nottingham and its Environs (1st edn 1866), A History of the Old Trent Bridge by M.O. Tarbotton (1871), A Souvenir of Newstead Abbey Formerly the Home of Lord Byron (1st edition 1874), and the Illustrated Great Midland Almanack. The Allens were also pioneering photographers. The company purchased a Beck Achromatic Stereoscope c.1860 and produced a series of 21 Photographic Views of Nottingham. Their Portret van Helena van Waldeck-Pyrmont met baby [Portrait of Helena van Waldeck-Pyrmont With Baby] c.1883–84 is in the collection of the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam.

The lease to Ashley House was assigned to John Archer Howitt of J. Howitt & Son Ltd, Alfred St. Mill, Nottingham in 1926. Notice of Assignment of lease May 1946 from Howitt to Col. E.E. Baker. In 1950 the Order of St. Clare requested permission to use the property for female students at the University; the City Council refused planning permission for institutional use. Notice of Assignment of lease October 1951, Baker to the War Department. The property was vacant from 20 August 1955 and was for sale by 1957 but no buyer was found. Offered by auction Thursday 27 March 1958. Notice of Assignment of lease 10 June 1958 from War Department to A.T. Horbury. Freehold purchased by A.T. Horbury Limited for £1,400 on 29 September 1958. Horbury submitted plan for Ashley Bungalow in 1963, by which time the main building had already been converted into 10 flats and 6 garages.

occupations.employer: FF and A Cleaver and Co., Wilford Road

people.bio: Mary Adelaide Allen (1841–1901) was the wife of Alfred Cleaver, whom she married in 1874. She was a daughter of the Allen family.

Robert Clifton Cleaver (b. 1868; d. 1 October 1900, Brandon, Manitoba, Canada) was the only son of Alfred Cleaver by his first wife Annie Bennett. He trained as an articled lawyer's clerk and later emigrated to Canada, where he died in Brandon, Manitoba.
Beatrice Mary Cleaver (b. c.1877) was the daughter of Alfred Cleaver and Mary Adelaide Allen (née Allen). She married Charles Richard Lamb in 1902 but was widowed by 1911.

Mary Adelaide Allen (1841–1901) married Alfred Cleaver in 1874. She was the daughter of Richard Allen and Mary Ann Deacon. Her father Richard owned Richard Allen & Son, a prominent Nottingham printer, bookseller and pioneering photographer based at Caxton House, 34 Long Row East.

Richard Allen (b. Leicester, 1814; d. Nottingham, 1884) was a prominent Nottingham printer, bookseller, photographer and publisher. He owned Richard Allen & Son, based at Caxton House, 34 Long Row East — one of Nottingham's leading printing and publishing firms. They were also pioneering photographers, purchasing a Beck Achromatic Stereoscope c.1860 and producing 21 Photographic Views of Nottingham. Their portrait of Helena van Waldeck-Pyrmont with baby (c.1883–84) is in the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam. The Allen family resided at Albert Villa, 21 Cavendish Crescent South, The Park.

occupations.employer: Richard Allen & Son, 34 Long Row East
